ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific stir-fried black pepper squid entry exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers squids and cuttlefishes as a category; no prepared stir-fried squid dish code exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for stir-fried black pepper squid dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fry black pepper squid is approximately 250 g, representing a shared zi char dish for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with stir-fry cooking fat and black pepper sauce additions estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base squid nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked squid (FDC ID 175177). Fat, sodium, and carbohydrate values adjusted upward to account for stir-fry cooking oil (~10 g oil per 100 g dish), black pepper sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, butter), and aromatics, consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood dish methodology. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — protein- and fat-rich seafood dishes; stir-fried squid estimated by analogy with low-GI cooked seafood and mixed stir-fry dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried black pepper squid. GI estimated at 30 by analogy with cooked seafood and mixed protein-dominant stir-fry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is protein- and fat-dominant with low net carbohydrate content (~5.2 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 13.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and black pepper sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fry black pepper squid has a low estimated GI of 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.9 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content and moderate saturated fat from butter and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~185 mg/100 g) and the dish contains moderate saturated fat from butter and stir-fry oil; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer preparations with less butter and reduced sodium sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fry Black Pepper Squid (黑椒炒鱿鱼; Sotong Goreng Lada Hitam) is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char dish in which fresh squid rings and tentacles are wok-fried at high heat with a bold black pepper sauce comprising cracked black pepper, butter, garlic, onion, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is characterised by a smoky, pungent, and slightly spicy flavour profile with a tender yet lightly chewy squid texture. It is a staple of Chinese-Malaysian seafood restaurants and zi char stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (~14.5 g/100 g), vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the squid, while the sauce contributes significant sodium (~520 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (~7.8 g/100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management when not paired with large portions of white rice. The high sodium content is the primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-suitable and contains mollusc and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fry squid?
Is black pepper squid good for diabetics?
What is sotong goreng lada hitam?
Is zi char squid high in cholesterol?
What is hei jiao chao you yu?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for stir-fry black pepper squid exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid data with sauce and cooking fat adjustments consistent with IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood methodology. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database seafood and mixed stir-fry references. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry or IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried seafood analogues. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this dish type. All other -1 fields represent nutrients not reported in the adapted source combination. No core macro is zero; all macros are positive and nutritionally plausible for a protein-fat-dominant stir-fried seafood dish. GI estimated at 30 by analogy with low-GI cooked seafood dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (30 × 13.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.9. Halal status assumed based on standard zi char preparation without pork ingredients; individual restaurant practices may vary. Allergens include molluscs and soy (oyster sauce, soy sauce); gluten may be present from oyster sauce. Cholesterol content is naturally elevated in squid as a cephalopod mollusc.
